section.title: The New Life in Christ
section.sectionRationale: Having freed the Colossians from human regulations, Paul now shows the true shape of Christian living, grounded not in rules but in union with the risen Christ. Because they have died and been raised with Christ and their life is hidden with Him in God, they are to seek the things above, put to death the old self, and put on the new self, clothing themselves in Christlike virtues, with love binding all together and the peace and word of Christ ruling their common life. This is the positive answer to legalism: real transformation flowing from union with Christ.
section.thesis: Because believers have been raised with Christ and their life is hidden with Him in God, they are to set their minds above, put to death the old self and put on the new, clothing themselves in compassion, forgiveness, and love, letting the peace and word of Christ rule so that everything is done in His name with thanksgiving.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the foundation, raised with Christ and hidden with Him in God, set your minds above; putting off the old self, the vices of the earthly nature; and putting on the new self, Christlike virtues bound by love, with the peace and word of Christ ruling the community, all done in His name with thanks.

units.label: Raised with Christ
units.note: your life is hidden with Christ in God

verses.text: Therefore, since you have been raised with Christ, strive for the things above, where Christ is seated at the right hand of God.
Set your minds on things above, not on earthly things.
For you died, and your life is now {{krypto|hidden}} with Christ in God.
When Christ, who is your life, appears, then you also will appear with Him in glory.

units.label: Put off the old self
units.note: put to death the earthly nature

verses.text: Put to death, therefore, the components of your earthly nature: sexual immorality, impurity, lust, evil desires, and greed, which is idolatry.
Because of these, the wrath of God is coming on the sons of disobedience.
When you lived among them, you also used to walk in these ways.
But now you must put aside all such things as these: anger, rage, malice, slander, and filthy language from your lips.
Do not lie to one another, since you have taken off the old self with its practices,

units.label: Put on the new self
units.note: clothed in virtue, bound by love, ruled by peace

verses.text: and have put on the new self, which is being renewed in knowledge in the {{eikon|image}} of its Creator.
Here there is no Greek or Jew, circumcised or uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave, or free, but Christ is all and is in all.
Therefore, as the elect of God, holy and beloved, clothe yourselves with hearts of compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness, and patience.
Bear with one another and forgive any complaint you may have against someone else. Forgive as the Lord forgave you.
And over all these virtues put on {{agape|love}}, which is the bond of perfect unity.
Let the {{eirene|peace}} of Christ rule in your hearts, for to this you were called as members of one body. And be thankful.
Let the word of Christ richly dwell within you as you teach and admonish one another with all wisdom, and as you sing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs with gratitude in your hearts to God.
And whatever you do, in word or deed, do it all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through Him.

krypto.gloss: To hide, conceal, or keep safe. Paul says your life is now hidden with Christ in God, using a perfect tense that means it has been safely stored and remains so. The believer's true life is not on display or at risk in this world; it is kept secure with Christ and will be revealed in glory when He appears.

eikon.gloss: Image, likeness, a true representation. The new self is being renewed in the image of its Creator, restoring what was marred at the fall when humanity, made in God's image, turned away. Since Christ Himself is the image of the invisible God, 1:15, to be renewed in God's image is to be conformed to Christ.

agape.gloss: Self-giving love that seeks the good of others, the highest Christian virtue. Paul says to put love on over all the other virtues, for it is the bond that ties them together in perfect unity. Love is not one item on the list but the belt that holds the whole wardrobe of Christlike character in place.

eirene.gloss: Peace, wholeness, the settled well-being that comes from being reconciled to God. Paul tells the peace of Christ to rule, literally to act as umpire or referee, in their hearts and among them as one body. This peace, secured by the cross, is to govern the community's life and settle its disputes.

basic.context: After freeing the church from man-made rules, Paul shows what real Christian living looks like. It flows from union with the risen Christ. Because you died and rose with Him, and your true life is safely hidden with Him in God, aim your heart at the things above. Then get rid of the old ways, sexual sin, greed, anger, slander, and lies, and put on the new self, dressing in compassion, kindness, humility, patience, forgiveness, and above all love. Let Christ's peace and Christ's word govern your life together, and do everything in His name with thankfulness.
basic.hermeneutics: The whole ethic is grounded in indicative before imperative: because you have been raised with Christ, therefore live this way. Paul uses the picture of taking off dirty clothes and putting on clean ones to describe real, ongoing change. These commands are for all believers, and they are the true alternative to the empty rule-keeping of chapter two.
basic.originalLanguages: Your real life is krypto, hidden and kept safe, with Christ in God. The new self is renewed in the eikon, the image, of its Creator, which means being made like Christ, who is God's image. Over every virtue you are to put on agape, love, the bond that ties them together. And the eirene, the peace, of Christ is to rule, to act as umpire, in your hearts and your shared life.
basic.typology: Being renewed in the image of the Creator restores humanity made in God's image in Genesis, now reformed after Christ, the true image. The new community where there is no Jew or Greek, slave or free, fulfills God's promise to gather one people from all nations. Putting off and putting on echoes the priestly changing of garments, now applied to every believer.
basic.christ: Christ is the risen Lord seated at God's right hand, and He is our very life, so that when He appears we will appear with Him in glory. He is the image into which we are being remade. He is all and in all in the new community, and His forgiveness is the pattern for ours. His peace rules our hearts and His word dwells in us richly.
basic.god: God raised Christ and seats Him at His right hand, and it is with God that our life is hidden and safe. God is the Creator whose image is being restored in us, and the Father to whom we give thanks through Christ. He chooses a people, holy and beloved, and calls them into one reconciled body.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 4 gives the same picture of taking off the old self and putting on the new. Romans 6 grounds new life in dying and rising with Christ. And Galatians 3 declares that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free.
basic.oneStory: This is the renewal of humanity that the whole Bible anticipates: image-bearers, ruined at the fall, remade after Christ the true image, and gathered into one new people from every nation. The risen life of Christ now shapes a community marked by love, forgiveness, and peace, a preview of the glory to be revealed when He appears.

context.literary: The passage turns on the therefore of verse 1, drawing the practical conclusion from the death and resurrection with Christ set out in 2:12 and 20. It is built on the paired images of putting off the old self, verses 5 to 9, and putting on the new, verses 10 to 14, climaxing in love, peace, and the word of Christ. The repeated call to thankfulness, verses 15, 16, and 17, ties the section together and leads into the household code that follows.
context.historical: The vices Paul lists, sexual immorality, greed, anger, slander, and lying, were common in the surrounding Greco-Roman culture, and the virtues he commends, humility and gentleness among them, were not always prized in that world. The abolition of distinctions between Greek and Jew, barbarian and Scythian, slave and free spoke powerfully into a society sharply divided by ethnicity and status.
context.canonical: The put off and put on pattern parallels Ephesians 4:17 to 5:2, and the new-humanity formula echoes Galatians 3:28 and 1 Corinthians 12:13. The renewal in the image of the Creator ties back to Genesis 1 and forward to the promise that believers will be conformed to the image of Christ, Romans 8:29.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that genuine transformation flows from union with the risen Christ, not from human rules, and to direct that new life into concrete change: killing the old vices, clothing oneself in Christlike virtue, and letting love, peace, and the word of Christ govern the church's shared life to the glory of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 1 to 4 declare what is true of every believer united to Christ. The commands to put off vices and put on virtues are prescriptive for all Christians in every age. The specific vice and virtue lists are representative rather than exhaustive, illustrating the character of the old and new self.

debates.question: Are believers already renewed in God's image, or is renewal ongoing?

views.label: A completed act with ongoing renewal
views.case: Paul says they have put on the new self as an accomplished fact, yet that new self is being renewed, a present, continuing work of growth into Christ's likeness.

views.label: Primarily a present process
views.case: The stress falls on the daily renewing in knowledge after the image of the Creator, an ongoing transformation the commands then spell out.

debates.bearing: Both hold together the already and the not yet: the decisive change has happened in Christ, and it is being worked out progressively as believers live it.

study.originalLanguages: The foundation is that the believer's true life is krypto, hidden and safely stored, with Christ in God, a perfect-tense reality that cannot be lost and will be unveiled in glory when Christ appears. The new self is being renewed in the eikon, the image, of its Creator, which means restoration into the likeness of Christ, who is Himself the image of the invisible God. The wardrobe of virtues, compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness, patience, and forgiveness, is crowned by agape, love, the outer garment that binds all the rest into perfect unity. And the eirene, the peace, of Christ is to rule, a word drawn from the arbiter who decides a contest, so that Christ's peace umpires both the individual heart and the life of the one body.

typology.type: Renewal in the image of the Creator
typology.fulfillment: Restores humanity made in God's image at creation, now reformed after Christ, the true image, reversing the marring of the fall.

typology.type: One new people from all nations
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills God's promise to bless all nations in Abraham, gathering Jew and Greek, slave and free into one body where Christ is all and in all.

typology.type: Putting off and putting on garments
typology.fulfillment: Echoes the priestly changing of garments and the exchange of defiled clothing for clean, now applied to every believer clothed in Christlike virtue.

study.christ: Christ dominates this new life at every point. He is risen and seated at the right hand of God, the place of authority, and the believer has been raised with Him. He is our very life, so hidden now, but when He appears we too will appear with Him in glory. He is the image into which the new self is being remade, and in the new community He is all and in all, dissolving every division. His forgiveness is the measure of ours, forgive as the Lord forgave you, His peace is to rule our hearts, and His word is to dwell in us richly. To live the new life is simply to live out of union with Christ.
study.god: God raised Christ and seated Him at His own right hand, and it is with God that the believer's life is now hidden and secure. God is the Creator whose image is being renewed in His people, and the electing God who has made them holy and beloved. He is the Father to whom all worship and thanks finally rise, through the Lord Jesus. In this passage God is both the safe keeper of our true life and the goal of our renewed life, glorified in a people remade after His image.

crossReferences.note: The same put off the old self and put on the new, created after God in righteousness and holiness.
New life grounded in dying and rising with Christ, dead to sin and alive to God.
In Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, matching verse 11.
God's purpose to conform believers to the image of His Son, the renewal Paul describes.

study.oneStory: This passage stages the renewal of humanity that the whole Bible awaits: image-bearers ruined at the fall are remade after Christ, the true image of God, and gathered into one new people from every nation and status. The risen life of Christ now reshapes a community marked by love, forgiveness, and peace, doing everything in His name with thanks. It is a foretaste of the glory that will be revealed when Christ, who is our life, appears.

quiz.q: On what does Paul ground the call to seek the things above?

options.text: That believers have been raised with Christ and their life is hidden with Him in God.
options.note: Verses 1 to 3 root the new life in union with the risen Christ.

options.text: That believers must earn heaven by strict effort.
options.note: The command flows from grace already received, not from earning.

options.text: That earthly life has no value whatsoever.
options.note: Paul redirects the mind's focus; he does not despise all earthly life.

options.text: That believers should withdraw from the world entirely.
options.note: The passage calls for transformed living within the world, not withdrawal.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by putting to death the old self?

options.text: Actively renouncing sins like immorality, greed, anger, slander, and lying.
options.note: Verses 5 to 9 name concrete vices to put to death and put aside.

options.text: Feeling occasional regret without changing.
options.note: Paul calls for real removal of these sins, not mere regret.

options.text: Keeping a list of outward rules like chapter two.
options.note: This is not rule-keeping but Spirit-shaped transformation.

options.text: Withdrawing into harsh treatment of the body.
options.note: That asceticism was rejected in 2:23; here the change is inward and moral.

quiz.q: Into whose image is the new self being renewed?

options.text: The image of its Creator, that is, into the likeness of Christ.
options.note: Verse 10 ties renewal to the Creator's image, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: The image of the surrounding culture.
options.note: The pattern is God's image in Christ, not the culture.

options.text: The image of the false teachers' ideal.
options.note: Renewal is after the Creator, not any human ideal.

options.text: No particular image; it is undefined.
options.note: It is clearly the image of the Creator, restored in Christ.

quiz.q: What does Paul say holds all the virtues together?

options.text: Love, which is the bond of perfect unity.
options.note: Verse 14 crowns the virtues with love as the binding garment.

options.text: Strict rule-keeping.
options.note: The binding virtue is love, not rules.

options.text: Personal ambition.
options.note: Ambition divides; love unites the body.

options.text: Fear of punishment.
options.note: It is love, not fear, that binds the virtues in unity.

quiz.q: What is to rule in the hearts and life of the church?

options.text: The peace of Christ, acting as umpire among members of one body.
options.note: Verse 15 lets Christ's peace govern the community's life.

options.text: Personal preference and majority opinion.
options.note: It is Christ's peace, not mere preference, that is to rule.

options.text: The demands of the false teachers.
options.note: Those demands were rejected; Christ's peace rules instead.

options.text: Constant competition for status.
options.note: Peace, not rivalry, is to govern the one body.

quiz.q: How does Paul say we should do everything, in word or deed?

options.text: In the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through Him.
options.note: Verse 17 sums up the new life: all done in Christ's name with thanks.

options.text: For our own reputation and gain.
options.note: All is to be done in Jesus name, not for self-promotion.

options.text: Only when others are watching.
options.note: Sincerity toward the Lord, not performance, marks the new self.

options.text: According to whatever feels right in the moment.
options.note: The measure is the name of the Lord Jesus, not passing feelings.

questions: Your true life is hidden with Christ in God, safe and secure. How would resting in that security change the way you handle pressure and fear this week?
Paul calls you to set your mind on things above. What earthly things tend to capture your attention, and how could you deliberately lift your gaze to Christ?
Putting off the old self is active, not passive. Which specific sin is God calling you to put to death right now, and what would that look like in practice?
You are told to forgive as the Lord forgave you. Is there a complaint or grievance you are holding that Christ is calling you to release?
Everything is to be done in the name of the Lord Jesus with thanks. How might naming Christ over your ordinary work and words reshape an average day?

translationNotes.note: Hidden renders a perfect tense, your life has been and remains safely stored with Christ in God, stressing security rather than concealment from view.
Some manuscripts read our life rather than your life; either way Christ Himself is named as the believer's life.
Rule translates a word for acting as umpire or arbiter; the peace of Christ is to decide and govern within the heart and the body.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Colossians 3:1-17. No em or en dashes appear in this passage.
